caption: Fig. 1. Schematic diagram of air induction melting with flux cover (a) during melting and (b) during pouring.
paper_abstract: This article presents a part of the research work conducted in our laboratory to develop lightweight steels based on Fe-Al alloys containing 7 wt.% and 9 wt.% aluminum for construction of advanced lightweight ground transportation systems, such as automotive vehicles and heavy-haul truck, and for civil engineering construction, such as bridges, tunnels, and buildings. The melting and casting of sound, porosity-free ingots of Fe-Al-based alloys was accomplished by a newly developed cost-effective technique. The technique consists of using a special flux cover and proprietary charging schedule during air induction melting. These alloys were also produced using a vacuum induction melting (VIM) process for comparison purposes. The effect of aluminum (7 wt.% and 9 wt.%) on melting, processing, and properties of disordered solid solution Fe-Al alloys has been studied in detail. Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy could be produced using air induction melting with a flux cover with the properties comparable to the alloy produced through the VIM route. This material could be further processed through hot and cold working to produce sheets and thin foils. The cold-rolled and annealed sheet exhibited excellent room-temperature ductility. The role of carbon in Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys has also been examined. The results indicate that Fe-Al and Fe-Al-C alloys containing about 7 wt.% Al are potential lightweight steels.
paper_title: Melting, Processing, and Properties of Disordered Fe-Al and Fe-Al-C Based Alloys
citation_sentences: Although the VIM process is known to yield Fe-Al alloy ingots free from gas porosity, the process is expensive and not easily available. Hence, efforts have been put into develop an alternative costeffective melting process to produce Fe-Al alloys without gas porosity. The alternative process involves air induction melting using a protective flux cover and proprietary charging schedule. Sound, porosity-free Fe-Al alloys ingots containing 7 wt.% and 9 wt.% Al were successfully produced by this process (Fig. 1). The 30-kg heats of Fe-Al alloys containing 7 wt.% and 9 wt.% Al were prepared by Inductotherm medium-frequency AIM of 50-kg capacity. Commercial purity aluminum and iron were used as raw material. The iron charge was cleaned by pickling in dilute hydrochloric acid. The iron pieces were charged into an alumina-lined crucible. After melting the iron charge, preheated flux containing $\mathrm { { A l } _ { 2 } \mathrm { { O } _ { 3 } , } }$ CaO, MgO, and $\mathrm { \bar { C } a F _ { 2 } }$ was added to provide a protective cover to the melt and was allowed to melt. The cleaned and preheated aluminum pieces were then plunged into the molten iron bath. The bath was held at 1900 K for a very short time (2 min) to prevent aluminum losses. The melt was then top poured into split cast iron molds. The flux cover added after melting iron charge acts as a barrier between the aluminum in the melt and water vapor in the air and prevents the formation and pick-up of hydrogen by the reaction between aluminum in the melt and water vapor in the air. The composition of the flux cover was optimized carefully to ensure that it refines the liquid metal with respect to impurities and that it does not attack the crucible lining. During AIM, the flux must be kept molten to allow charging of aluminum and to aid pouring into molds. The flux does not take part in the heat generation and is kept molten through the heat transfer from the molten metal beneath and by the heat released due to the highly exothermic reaction between iron and aluminum. Therefore, the flux charge had to be limited to an amount that is just sufficient to provide adequate protective cover during charging and melting of aluminum.

citation_sentences: Selection of crucible lining material for melting iron aluminides is very critical in the above three processes. This is important for two reasons: (i) to have a long crucible life and (ii) to minimize impurity pick-up from the crucible lining. MgO is the most common choice for commercial steels and alloys containing a low level of aluminum. The standard free energy data (Fig. 2) show that Al in the melt can reduce MgO at temperatures of about 1873 K, which is very close to the melting point of iron aluminides.21 The reduction of MgO results in the pick-up of magnesium by the alloy and in erosion of the crucible. It has also been reported that high Mg levels especially when segregated can cause problems in hot working.19 Ingots of both Fe-7 wt.% and 9 wt.% Al alloy were completely free from gas porosity. The Fe-7Al alloy ingots were completely free from microcracks, whereas Fe-9Al alloy ingots exhibited microcracks. These microcracks are the result of hydrogen embrittlement due to the presence of a higher concentration of hydrogen (Table I) present in these alloys.

caption: Fig. 3. Cold-rolled Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy (a) plates and sheet and (b) foils produced by the AIMFC process.
paper_abstract: This article presents a part of the research work conducted in our laboratory to develop lightweight steels based on Fe-Al alloys containing 7 wt.% and 9 wt.% aluminum for construction of advanced lightweight ground transportation systems, such as automotive vehicles and heavy-haul truck, and for civil engineering construction, such as bridges, tunnels, and buildings. The melting and casting of sound, porosity-free ingots of Fe-Al-based alloys was accomplished by a newly developed cost-effective technique. The technique consists of using a special flux cover and proprietary charging schedule during air induction melting. These alloys were also produced using a vacuum induction melting (VIM) process for comparison purposes. The effect of aluminum (7 wt.% and 9 wt.%) on melting, processing, and properties of disordered solid solution Fe-Al alloys has been studied in detail. Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy could be produced using air induction melting with a flux cover with the properties comparable to the alloy produced through the VIM route. This material could be further processed through hot and cold working to produce sheets and thin foils. The cold-rolled and annealed sheet exhibited excellent room-temperature ductility. The role of carbon in Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys has also been examined. The results indicate that Fe-Al and Fe-Al-C alloys containing about 7 wt.% Al are potential lightweight steels.
paper_title: Melting, Processing, and Properties of Disordered Fe-Al and Fe-Al-C Based Alloys
citation_sentences: The 80-mm-diameter AIM, VIM, and AIMFC ingots of Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy and VIM and AIMFC ingots of 9 wt.% Al alloys were preheated in a hearth furnace at 1373 K for 1 h and hot-forged in a 1- tonne pneumatic forge hammer with die platens at room temperature. The 24-mm-thick sections of the forged billets were preheated at 1373 K for 1 h and hot rolled by 50% with 1-mm reduction in thickness per pass. The samples were reheated after every six rolling passes under similar conditions as in preheating. The final thickness of the hot-rolled plate was 12 mm. Ingots of Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys produced by all three processes exhibited excellent hot workability. The Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy by VIM and AIMFC could also be cold rolled to thin sheets and foils (Fig. 3). Due to the presence of gas porosity, AIM ingots containing 9 wt.% Al could not be thermomechanically processed. The hot-working response of Fe-9 wt.% Al alloy produced by AIMFC was poor. This is in contrast to the excellent hot workability of the same alloy produced by VIM. Microcracks were observed in hot-rolled plates. These cracks may be due to the presence of a high level of residual hydrogen in the as-cast ingots (Table I), and they may persist even after hot working. All alloys produced by the AIM, AIMFC, and VIM process exhibited an annealed microstructure with coarse grain size (Fig. 4). The grain size of all alloys produced by AIMFC is comparable with the grain size of alloys produced by the VIM process (Table II).

caption: Fig. 4. Optical micrographs of hot-rolled Fe-Al alloys containing (a) AIM 7 wt.% Al, (b) AIMFC 7 wt.% Al, (c) AIMFC 9 wt.% Al, (d) VIM 7 wt.% Al, and (e) VIM 9 wt.% Al.

citation_sentences: The ingots were hot forged and hot rolled to 12 mm thickness. The microstructure of the alloy with 0.02 C (Fig. 5) showed a single-phase structure with no precipitates. On the contrary, alloy containing higher levels of carbon exhibited significant amounts of precipitates. These precipitates were confirmed to be $\mathrm { F i e _ { 3 } A l C _ { 0 . 5 } }$ by x-ray diffraction analysis (Fig. 6) using a Philips 3710 x-ray diffractometer. The volume fraction of these precipitates increases significantly with increasing carbon content. The room-temperature tensile properties with different carbon are given in Table V.

caption: Fig. 6. XRD traces using Cu-Ka radiation showing Fe3Al peaks in (a) Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.012 wt.% C; Fe3Al and $\mathsf { F e } _ { 3 } \mathsf { A l C } _ { 0 . 5 }$ peaks in (b) Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.15 wt.% C. Similar peaks were observed in three other alloys with carbon 0.35–1 wt.%.
